---
title: "BEAR"
subtitle: "Benchmarks of Empirical Accuracy in Research"
---

![](assets/bear_banner.png){.bear-banner fig-alt="BEAR banner"}


::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## Problem

Quantitative metascience drives debates about important issues such as 
publication bias, p-hacking, reporting practices, pre-registration, significance rates, and more. 
Most of the crucial contributions of this branch of metascience has been 
based on analysing individual datasets, often painstakingly constructed by researchers.
But how generalisable are these findings?

:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## Solution

BEAR is a open-source database for metascience exploration
with 25 datasets across many scientific fields and millions of data points. 
BEAR does not generate any new source data; it repackages public datasets 
into a common structure for research on replication, exchangeability, 
meta-analysis, and other questions about standards of empirical research.
:::
:::


::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
To download latest version of data, go to
[GitHub Releases](https://github.com/wwiecek/BEAR/releases). 
or you can follow [download instructions](getting-started.qmd) for command line.
:::

::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## Datasets

BEAR combines curated registries of studies, random collections of articles in different disciplines, sets of meta-analyses, and direct replication projects. It covers data in econonmics, political science, biomedicine, ecology, evolution, education, neuroscience, and more. Head to [datasets section](datasets.qmd) to read about individual datasets.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## What to do with these data

At a minimum BEAR contains z-values with clearly labelled identifiers for studies
and meta-analyses. Where available, effect sizes, standard errors, sample sizes,
and additional covariates (e.g. pre-registration status, phase of clinical trial,
journal, discipline) are also included.
An optional model of signal-to-noise ratios (based on van Zwet et al) 
is also inlcuded as part of the repository, to quantify selection, 
power, and replicability of data. 
:::
:::

## Links

- [Get data](getting-started.qmd) for a minimal R example.
- [Datasets](datasets.qmd) for the current dataset index, basic results, and individual
  dataset descriptions.
- [Reproduce](reproduce.qmd) for more complete workflow of rebuilding the database
  from individual data sources and modelling.
- [GitHub repository](https://github.com/wwiecek/BEAR) to access code and dataset 
  directly.
